title: Chapter 1.5 - Theory of Consumer Behaviour (Part V) - Income and Substitution Effects & Revealed Preference
description: A challenging exam-standard quiz assessing conceptual, analytical, and applied reasoning for the Income, Substitution, and Total Effects, and the core principles of Revealed Preference Theory.
subject: BEC1370 - Microeconomics

questions.question: Which variable is explicitly held constant when isolating the Substitution Effect, causing the consumer's movement to be along the original indifference curve?
questions.rationale: The Substitution Effect is isolated by adjusting money income to keep the consumer on the original indifference curve, thus holding utility constant.

questions.question: If the price of a normal good increases, what is the combined directional impact of the Substitution Effect (SE) and Income Effect (IE) on the quantity demanded?
options: SE decreases Q, IE increases Q
SE increases Q, IE decreases Q
Both SE and IE decrease Q
Both SE and IE increase Q
questions.answer: Both SE and IE decrease Q
questions.rationale: For a normal good, a price increase causes SE to decrease Q (it's relatively more expensive) and IE to decrease Q (real income falls).

questions.question: For a standard inferior good, which statement accurately describes the relationship between the two effects when the good's price falls?
options: IE increases Q, SE increases Q, Total Effect is large
IE decreases Q, SE decreases Q, Total Effect is small
IE decreases Q, SE increases Q, SE is larger than the magnitude of IE
IE increases Q, SE decreases Q, IE is larger than the magnitude of SE
questions.answer: IE decreases Q, SE increases Q, SE is larger than the magnitude of IE
questions.rationale: A price fall for an inferior good leads to an increase in real income, causing the negative IE (buy less). However, the positive SE (buy more, as it is relatively cheaper) is typically stronger, resulting in an overall increase in quantity demanded.

questions.question: The Income Effect is best described as the change in quantity demanded resulting from a change in the consumer's:
questions.rationale: The Income Effect captures the change in purchasing power due to the price change, which shifts the budget line and, consequently, the utility level.

questions.question: The nature of the Substitution Effect dictates that a consumer will always substitute a less expensive good for a more expensive one, meaning SE and price change have a:
questions.rationale: When price increases, SE decreases quantity demanded, and when price decreases, SE increases quantity demanded, which is a negative relationship.

questions.question: What is the unique characteristic of a Giffen good that results in a violation of the Law of Demand (a positively sloped demand curve)?
options: SE is greater than the negative IE
IE and SE work in the same direction
The negative IE is greater than the positive SE in magnitude
The good has zero income elasticity
questions.answer: The negative IE is greater than the positive SE in magnitude
questions.rationale: For a Giffen good, the strong negative Income Effect outweighs the positive Substitution Effect, causing the Total Effect to be negative when price falls (i.e., less is purchased).

questions.question: When decomposing the Total Effect, the imaginary budget constraint (B star) is drawn parallel to the new budget constraint (B2) to ensure that which factor is constant for the Income Effect analysis?
questions.rationale: B star is parallel to B2, meaning it reflects the new, lower price ratio, thus holding the relative prices constant for the second step, the Income Effect (movement from C to B).

questions.question: The core principle of the Revealed Preference Theory (RPT) is that a consumer's true preferences can be inferred by observing their:
questions.rationale: RPT focuses on observing real-world consumption decisions (revealed preference) rather than asking direct questions (stated preference).

questions.question: According to RPT, if a consumer purchases basket A rather than basket B, even though A is not cheaper than B, the inference is that the consumer:
questions.rationale: RPT infers preference from the observed choice: if A is chosen when B was available and affordable, A is preferred.

questions.question: When analyzing the Income Effect (IE), the movement from point C to point B (from U1 to U2) signifies a change in:
questions.rationale: The IE moves the consumer to a new indifference curve (IC), which represents a different utility level, due to the change in real income.

questions.question: The Total Effect of a price change on the quantity demanded is defined by the formula:
options: Real Income change plus Money Income change
Relative Price change minus Purchasing Power change
questions.rationale: Total Effect = Substitution Effect + Income Effect.

questions.question: By definition, all goods classified as Giffen goods are also required to be classified as:
questions.rationale: A Giffen good is a special case of an inferior good where the negative income effect dominates the substitution effect.

questions.question: The Substitution Effect is graphically represented as a movement along a single indifference curve because the decomposition methodology holds the consumer's:
questions.rationale: The definition of the Substitution Effect is the change in quantity demanded while holding utility constant, which is shown by a movement along the same indifference curve.

questions.question: Which set of economists are credited with developing the theory of revealed preference?
options: John Maynard Keynes and Milton Friedman
questions.rationale: The theory of revealed preference was developed by Paul Samuelson and John Hicks.

questions.question: When the price of a normal good falls, the resulting increase in real income leads to the Income Effect causing the consumer to buy:
options: The same amount of the good
questions.rationale: For a normal good, an increase in real income leads to an increase in quantity demanded, which is the nature of its Income Effect.

questions.question: The decomposition of the Total Effect into the Substitution Effect and Income Effect is necessary because a price change simultaneously alters both the consumer's relative prices and their ________.
questions.rationale: A price change alters relative prices (SE) and the consumer's purchasing power (IE), which is their real income.

questions.question: The Income Effect causes a shift to another indifference curve, and its direction is dependent on the good's ________.
questions.rationale: The nature of the good, defined by its income elasticity (normal, inferior, Giffen), determines the direction of the Income Effect.

questions.question: When the price of good X increases, the Substitution Effect will cause the quantity demanded of X to ________, regardless of whether X is a normal or inferior good.
questions.rationale: The Substitution Effect is always negative, meaning a price increase always leads to a decrease in quantity demanded as the good becomes relatively more expensive.

questions.question: Which of the following statements are always true regarding the Substitution Effect (SE) of a price change?
options: It results in a change in total utility.
It has a negative relationship with the price change.
It is isolated by holding money income constant.
It represents a movement along the original indifference curve.
answer: It has a negative relationship with the price change.
It represents a movement along the original indifference curve.
questions.rationale: SE is always negative (causes a decrease in quantity when price rises) and is defined as the change while holding utility constant, which is a movement along the IC.

questions.question: Which of the following conditions must hold true for a good to be classified as a Giffen Good when its price falls?
options: The good must be a luxury good.
The good must be an inferior good.
The negative Income Effect must be stronger in magnitude than the positive Substitution Effect.
The demand curve must be downward sloping.
answer: The good must be an inferior good.
The negative Income Effect must be stronger in magnitude than the positive Substitution Effect.
questions.rationale: A Giffen good is an inferior good where the negative Income Effect (IE) is so strong that it outweighs the positive Substitution Effect (SE), causing the quantity demanded to fall when the price falls.

questions.question: Which of the following are characteristics that differentiate the Ordinal Utility Theory's stated preference from the Revealed Preference Theory's approach?
options: Stated preference relies on hypothetical choices or direct inquiry.
Revealed preference derives preferences from actual market choices.
Stated preference is always a cheaper and faster method for obtaining reliable data.
Revealed preference needs direct inquiry about preferences.
answer: Stated preference relies on hypothetical choices or direct inquiry.
Revealed preference derives preferences from actual market choices.
questions.rationale: Stated preference uses hypothetical questions; Revealed preference uses observed, actual choices. Stated preference is noted as difficult and time-consuming, and RPT avoids direct inquiry.

questions.question: In the graphical decomposition of a price fall for a good, the move from the initial equilibrium to the tangency point on the original indifference curve represents the [dropdown_1] and occurs along the [dropdown_2] budget line.
questions.rationale: The Substitution Effect is the movement from the initial equilibrium (A) to the imaginary tangency point (C) on the original indifference curve (U1).

questions.question: For a [dropdown_1] good, the Income Effect and Substitution Effect both lead to an [dropdown_2] in quantity demanded following a price fall.
questions.rationale: For normal goods, a price fall makes the good relatively cheaper (SE increases Q) and increases real income (IE increases Q). Both effects increase quantity demanded.

questions.question: The Substitution Effect always has a [dropdown_1] relationship with the price of a good, whereas the Income Effect's relationship with price depends on the good's [dropdown_2].
questions.rationale: The Substitution Effect is always negative. The Income Effect's direction (positive for normal, negative for inferior/Giffen) depends on the good's income elasticity.

questions.question: Match the type of good and effect to its characteristic directional outcome when the price of the good falls.
Match the economic theory with its primary method of deriving consumer preferences.

matchPairs.right: Actual market behavior and observed choices

questions.question: Match the effect to the correct representation of the consumer's equilibrium shift on an Indifference Map.

matchPairs.right: Movement along a single indifference curve
Shift to a different indifference curve
Movement from the initial to the final equilibrium

questions.question: A consumer experiences a significant drop in the price of their staple food, 'X'. Despite this, their purchase of 'X' decreases. Explain the microeconomic conditions (in terms of Income and Substitution Effects) that must be true for good 'X' to exhibit this counter-intuitive behavior.

Distinguish clearly between a typical Inferior Good and a Giffen Good based on the magnitude and direction of their Income and Substitution Effects, particularly when the price of the good falls. Use the Total Effect formula (TE = SE + IE) to illustrate the difference in the final consumption outcome.

Explain how the principles of the Revealed Preference Theory (RPT) are utilized in modern Big Data Analysis by online companies (like Amazon or YouTube) for making customer recommendations. Contrast this practical approach with the 'Stated Preference' method traditionally used in the older Ordinal Utility Theory.
